params.text:
The seven data protection principles

UK GDPR is built on seven principles. They are not optional extras — they shape every decision you make about personal information.

  1. Lawfulness, fairness and transparency – have a valid reason to use the information, be fair, and be open about what you do with it.
  2. Purpose limitation – only use information for the specific purpose it was collected for.
  3. Data minimisation – collect only what you actually need, nothing more.
  4. Accuracy – keep records correct and up to date; correct mistakes promptly.
  5. Storage limitation – do not keep information for longer than necessary.
  6. Integrity and confidentiality (security) – keep information safe from loss, damage or unauthorised access.
  7. Accountability – be able to show that you are meeting all the other principles.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Match each principle to what it means in your everyday work.

dialogs.answer: Only record what you genuinely need. Don't note a person's religion or a family member's phone number 'just in case' if it isn't relevant to their care.
Information gathered to plan someone's care must not be reused for an unrelated purpose, such as marketing, without a proper basis.
If a service user tells you their allergy or next of kin has changed, update the record straight away so the wrong information can't cause harm.
Old records shouldn't pile up forever. Follow your organisation's retention schedule and dispose of them securely when the time comes.
Your service must be able to prove it follows the rules — through policies, training, records and clear responsibilities.
